$$\text{BP}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)$$

**Model 2 — direct effect** (Exercise and WeightLoss):

$$\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(120,\, 10)$$ $$ 
\beta_{\text{ex}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 5) $$

$$\beta_{\text{wl}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 5) $$

$$\sigma \sim \text{HalfNormal}(10)$$

$$\mu_i = \alpha +  \underbrace{\beta_{\text{ex}}}_{\text{direct effect}} \cdot \text{Exercise}_i + \underbrace{\beta_{\text{wl}}}_{\text{mediated effect}} \cdot \text{WeightLoss}_i $$

$$\text{BP}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)$$

In Model 1, $\beta_{\text{ex}}$ captures the **total effect** of exercise: that is, everything that reaches blood pressure through any path, including through weight loss.

In Model 2, conditioning on `WeightLoss` blocks the mediated path, so $\beta_{\text{ex}}$ estimates only the **direct effect** that bypasses the mediator, and $\beta_{\text{wl}}$ isolates the weight-loss-to-blood-pressure relationship.

```{python}

# Model 1: total effect (no conditioning on WeightLoss) 
def total_effect_model(exercise, bp):
    alpha    = pyro.sample("alpha",   dist.Normal(120.0, 10.0))
    beta_ex  = pyro.sample("beta_ex", dist.Normal(0.0,   5.0))
    sigma    = pyro.sample("sigma",   dist.HalfNormal(10.0))
    mu = alpha + beta_ex * exercise
    with pyro.plate("obs", len(bp)):
        pyro.sample("bp", dist.Normal(mu, sigma), obs=bp)


# Model 2: direct effect (conditioning on WeightLoss) 
def direct_effect_model(exercise, weight_loss, bp):
    alpha    = pyro.sample("alpha",   dist.Normal(120.0, 10.0))
    beta_ex  = pyro.sample("beta_ex", dist.Normal(0.0,   5.0))
    beta_wl  = pyro.sample("beta_wl", dist.Normal(0.0,   5.0))
    sigma    = pyro.sample("sigma",   dist.HalfNormal(10.0))
    mu = alpha + beta_ex * exercise + beta_wl * weight_loss
    with pyro.plate("obs", len(bp)):
        pyro.sample("bp", dist.Normal(mu, sigma), obs=bp)


def run_pipe_mcmc(model, *args, num_samples=1000, warmup_steps=500):
    pyro.clear_param_store()
    mcmc = MCMC(NUTS(model), num_samples=num_samples,
                warmup_steps=warmup_steps, disable_progbar=True)
    mcmc.run(*args)
    return mcmc.get_samples()


post_total_pipe  = run_pipe_mcmc(total_effect_model,  ex_t,       bp_t)
post_direct_pipe = run_pipe_mcmc(direct_effect_model, ex_t, wl_t, bp_t)
```

```{python}
draws_pipe = {
    "Total effect\n(Exercise -> BP)":          post_total_pipe["beta_ex"].numpy(),
    "Direct effect\n(Exercise -> BP | WL)":    post_direct_pipe["beta_ex"].numpy(),
    "Mediated path\n(WeightLoss -> BP | Ex.)": post_direct_pipe["beta_wl"].numpy(),
}
true_vals_pipe = {
    "Total effect\n(Exercise -> BP)":          -1.0,
    "Direct effect\n(Exercise -> BP | WL)":     0.0,
    "Mediated path\n(WeightLoss -> BP | Ex.)": -2.0,
}

plot_order_pipe = list(draws_pipe.keys())
all_pipe_vals   = np.concatenate(list(draws_pipe.values()))
x_lo_p, x_hi_p = np.quantile(all_pipe_vals, [0.005, 0.995])
x_pad_p         = 0.15 * (x_hi_p - x_lo_p)
x_grid_p        = np.linspace(x_lo_p - x_pad_p, x_hi_p + x_pad_p, 400)
ridge_h_p       = 0.72

pipe_rows, pipe_int_rows, pipe_summary_rows = [], [], []

for i, name in enumerate(plot_order_pipe):
    y_base = len(plot_order_pipe) - 1 - i
    draws  = draws_pipe[name]
    kde    = gaussian_kde(draws)
    dens   = kde(x_grid_p)
    dens_s = dens / dens.max() * ridge_h_p
    lo, hi = np.quantile(draws, [0.025, 0.975])
    median = np.median(draws)
    tv     = true_vals_pipe[name]

    for x, d in zip(x_grid_p, dens_s):
        pipe_rows.append({"label": name, "x": x, "y_base": y_base, "y": y_base + d})
        if lo <= x <= hi:
            pipe_int_rows.append({"label": name, "x": x, "y_base": y_base, "y": y_base + d})

    pipe_summary_rows.append({
        "label":    name,
        "y_base":   y_base,
        "median":   median,
        "true_val": tv,
        "y_top":    y_base + np.interp(median, x_grid_p, dens_s),
        "y_true_top": y_base + np.interp(tv, x_grid_p, dens_s),
    })


def build_polygon_pipe(df, order, col="label"):
    rows = []
    for name in order:
        sub = df[df[col] == name].sort_values("x")
        if len(sub) == 0:
            continue
        yb = sub["y_base"].iloc[0]
        rows.extend(sub.to_dict("records"))
        rows += [
            {col: name, "x": sub["x"].iloc[-1], "y_base": yb, "y": yb},
            {col: name, "x": sub["x"].iloc[0],  "y_base": yb, "y": yb},
        ]
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


pipe_df       = pd.DataFrame(pipe_rows)
pipe_int_df   = pd.DataFrame(pipe_int_rows)
pipe_poly     = build_polygon_pipe(pipe_df,     plot_order_pipe)
pipe_int_poly = build_polygon_pipe(pipe_int_df, plot_order_pipe)
pipe_sum_df   = pd.DataFrame(pipe_summary_rows)

p_pipe = (
    ggplot()
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="#d9d9d9", size=1.2)
    + geom_polygon(pipe_poly,     aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
                   fill="#c8dce8", alpha=0.75, color=None)
    + geom_polygon(pipe_int_poly, aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
                   fill="#8fb7cb", alpha=0.70, color=None)
    + geom_line(pipe_df, aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
                color="#002b63", size=1.2)
    + geom_segment(
        pipe_sum_df.assign(x_start=x_grid_p.min(), x_end=x_grid_p.max()),
        aes(x="x_start", xend="x_end", y="y_base", yend="y_base"),
        color="#002b63", size=1.0,
    )
    + geom_segment(
        pipe_sum_df,
        aes(x="median", xend="median", y="y_base", yend="y_top"),
        color="#4b8aa6", size=2.3,
    )
    + geom_segment(
        pipe_sum_df,
        aes(x="true_val", xend="true_val", y="y_base", yend="y_true_top"),
        color="#c1121f", size=2.3,
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        breaks=pipe_sum_df["y_base"].tolist(),
        labels=pipe_sum_df["label"].tolist(),
        expand=(0.06, 0.25),
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(expand=(0.02, 0.02))
    + coord_cartesian(ylim=(-0.55, len(plot_order_pipe) - 0.15))
    + labs(
        title="Total effect vs. direct effect: pipe structure",
        subtitle="blue tick = posterior median  ·  red tick = true value  ·  shaded = 95% interval",
        x="Coefficient estimate (mmHg)",
        y="",
    )
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10.5, 6.8),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill="white", color="white"),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="white", color="white"),
        panel_grid_major=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_line_x=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_line_y=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_ticks_major=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_ticks_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_text_x=element_text(size=12, family="serif", color="#333333"),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=11, family="serif", color="#333333", weight="bold"),
        axis_title_x=element_text(size=12, family="serif", color="#333333", margin={"t": 10}),
        plot_title=element_text(size=24, family="serif", color="black"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=17, family="serif", color="black"),
    )
)

p_pipe
```

The fitted posteriors make a clear distinction between **total effect**, **direct effect**, and **mediated path**.

For the **total effect** of `Exercise` on `Blood pressure`, the posterior median sits very close to the true value of $-1$ mmHg. This is the quantity we recover when we do **not** condition on `WeightLoss`, because we want to keep the whole causal route

$$\text{Exercise} \rightarrow \text{WeightLoss} \rightarrow \text{Blood pressure}$$

inside the estimand. In other words, this model asks for the overall effect of `Exercise` on `Blood pressure`, including the part that works through `WeightLoss`.

For the **direct effect** of `Exercise` on `Blood pressure` conditional on `WeightLoss`, the posterior is much more uncertain and is centred somewhat to the right of zero. But the 95% interval still includes $0$, which is the true direct effect in this simulation. This is exactly the question:

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
If two people had the same `WeightLoss`, would `Exercise` still predict a difference in `Blood pressure`?
:::

In the data-generating process used here, the answer is no. So once we condition on `WeightLoss`, the remaining exercise coefficient should be interpreted as a noisy estimate of a direct effect that is truly absent.

For the **mediated path** coefficient, the posterior is tight and close to the true value of $-2$ mmHg. This coefficient measures the relationship between `WeightLoss` and `Blood pressure` after accounting for `Exercise`. Because `WeightLoss` is a continuous predictor with a strong effect, the model learns this parameter much more precisely than either of the `Exercise` coefficients.

The key point is that the two `Exercise` coefficients are different because they answer different causal questions. Leaving `WeightLoss` out gives the **total effect** of `Exercise`. Putting `WeightLoss` into the model blocks the mediation route and gives the **direct effect** instead.

[Conditioning on the mediator does not make the model better or worse. It changes the question. If `WeightLoss` is part of the mechanism we want to measure, we should leave it out and estimate the total effect. If we want the part of the `Exercise` effect that does not run through `WeightLoss`, we should condition on it and estimate the direct effect.]{.highlight-key-point}
::::
::::::

The third is the **collider** structure:

![Collider structure.](img/collider_structure.png){#fig-collider-structure width="50%"}

In a collider, two arrows meet at the same node:

$$X \rightarrow Z \leftarrow Y$$

Here, $Z$ is a common **effect** of $X$ and $Y$. This is very different from a fork. In a fork, the middle node is a common cause and conditioning on it blocks a confounding path. In a collider, the middle node is a common effect and conditioning on it opens a path that was previously closed.

Without conditioning on $Z$, the path between $X$ and $Y$ is closed:

$$X \perp\!\!\!\perp Y$$

But once we condition on the collider, information can flow through it:

$$X \not\!\perp\!\!\!\perp Y \mid Z$$

This can create an association between $X$ and $Y$ even when neither causes the other. The association is not evidence of a causal relationship between the endpoints; it is a consequence of conditioning on a shared outcome. This is called **collider bias**; when the conditioning happens through sample inclusion, this is often called **selection bias**.

[The practical rule is: do not condition on a collider, or on a variable downstream of a collider, unless the estimand specifically requires it and the induced bias is understood.]{.highlight-key-point}

:::: {.callout-tip icon="false" collapse="true"}
## Intuitive example of a collider structure

Suppose a selective university admits students who are either smart or athletic, or some combination of the two. In the general population, assume intelligence and athleticism are unrelated: knowing that someone is smart tells us nothing about whether they are athletic.

The admission decision is therefore a shared effect:

$$    \text{Smart} \rightarrow \text{Admitted} \leftarrow \text{Athletic}$$

If we look only at admitted students, we have conditioned on the collider. This can create a negative association between intelligence and athleticism even though the two traits were independent in the wider population. Among admitted students, someone who is not athletic is more likely to be very smart, because they still had to clear the admissions threshold somehow.

This is the collider intuition: once we know the shared outcome occurred, learning that one possible cause is absent makes the other cause more plausible. The two causes begin to "explain away" each other.

[The observed trade-off is not evidence that intelligence reduces athleticism, or that athleticism reduces intelligence. It is a selection artifact created by conditioning on their shared outcome.]{.highlight-key-point}

::: {.callout-note icon="false" collapse="true"}
## Code for the collider example

Let's simulate the same collider structure using the university admissions example:

![Collider example DAG.](img/collider_example.png){#fig-collider-example width="75%"}

$$\text{Smart} \rightarrow \text{Admitted} \leftarrow \text{Athletic}$$

The important setup is that `Smart` and `Athletic` are uncorrelated traits in the full population. Here we draw both as continuous standard-normal variables and make them independent by construction:

```python
smart = rng_collider.normal(0, 1, n_population)
athletic = rng_collider.normal(0, 1, n_population)
```

Then we make `Admitted` a binary variable. A student is admitted if their combined smart and athletic scores are high enough. This makes admission a shared outcome of both traits, so `Admitted` is a collider:

```python
admission_score = smart + athletic
admission_threshold = np.quantile(admission_score, 0.8)
admitted = (admission_score >= admission_threshold).astype(int)
```
 Here is the full population data-generating process with `Admitted` as a collider:
```{python}
# Full population data-generating process 
rng_collider = np.random.default_rng(42)

n_population = 2000

smart = rng_collider.normal(0, 1, n_population)
athletic = rng_collider.normal(0, 1, n_population)

# Admission is binary, but both inputs are continuous.
admission_score = smart + athletic
admission_threshold = np.quantile(admission_score, 0.8)
admitted = (admission_score >= admission_threshold).astype(int)

population_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Smart": smart,
    "Athletic": athletic,
    "Admitted": admitted
})

population_df.head()
```

In the full population, `Smart` and `Athletic` are independent by construction. Knowing that someone has a high value of `Smart` tells us essentially nothing about their value of `Athletic`. The sample correlation should therefore be close to zero.

##### Summary of the simulated data

Let's plot the relationship between `Smart` and `Athletic` in the full population:

```{python}
from plotnine import (
    ggplot,
    aes,
    geom_point,
    geom_smooth,
    labs,
    theme_minimal,
    theme,
    element_text,
)

from plotnine import annotate

# Calculate the correlation
full_population_corr = population_df[["Smart", "Athletic"]].corr().iloc[0, 1]

(
    ggplot(population_df, aes(x="Smart", y="Athletic"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.35, size=1.2)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", se=False, color="#bf616a")
    + labs(
        title="Smart and athletic are unrelated in the full population",
        subtitle="The two traits were generated independently, so the fitted line is approximately flat.",
        x="Smart",
        y="Athletic"
    )
    + annotate(
        "text",
        x=population_df["Smart"].min() + 0.5,
        y=population_df["Athletic"].max() - 0.5,
        label=f"corr = {full_population_corr:.2f}",
        size=10,
        ha='left',
        va='top'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(6.5, 4.5),
        plot_title=element_text(weight="bold")
    )
)
```

Now suppose we only look at admitted students. This is equivalent to conditioning on the collider `Admitted`.

```{python}
# ── Selected sample: admitted students only 
admitted_df = population_df.query("Admitted == 1").copy()
```

Among admitted students, `Smart` and `Athletic` are no longer independent:

```{python}
# Calculate the correlation
admitted_population_corr = admitted_df[["Smart", "Athletic"]].corr().iloc[0, 1]

(
    ggplot(admitted_df, aes(x="Smart", y="Athletic"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.35, size=1.2)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", se=False, color="#bf616a")
    + labs(
        title="Smart and athletic are associated in the admitted students sample",
        subtitle="The two traits are associated in the admitted students sample,\nbut were independent in the full population.",
        x="Smart",
        y="Athletic"
    )
    + annotate(
        "text",
        x=admitted_df["Smart"].min() + 0.1,  # Top-left, padding from left edge
        y=admitted_df["Athletic"].max() - 0.1,  # Slightly below top edge
        label=f"corr = {admitted_population_corr:.2f}",
        size=10,
        color="black",
        ha='left',
        va='top'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(6.5, 4.5),
        plot_title=element_text(weight="bold")
    )
)
```

The reason is selection. In the admitted group, a student with a lower value of `Smart` usually needs a higher value of `Athletic` to clear the admission threshold. Similarly, a less athletic admitted student usually needs to be smarter. The two traits start to trade off against each other inside the selected sample, even though no such trade-off exists in the full population.

So the key point is:

$$\text{Smart} \perp\!\!\!\perp \text{Athletic}$$

in the full population, but

$$  
\text{Smart} \not\!\perp\!\!\!\perp \text{Athletic} \mid \text{Admitted}$$

after conditioning on admission.

##### Analyze the data to see the associations

So, should we condition on the collider? Usually, **no**. If the goal is to learn the relationship between the two causes, `Smart` and `Athletic`, in the population, conditioning on `Admitted` would create an association that is not causal.

In the regression examples in this post, conditioning on a variable means including it as a covariate in the model. So here, conditioning on `Admitted` means adding `Admitted` to the linear predictor.

We fit two Bayesian linear regressions: one unconditioned model and one regression-adjusted model that includes `Admitted`.

**Model 1 - no conditioning on the collider**:

$$\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{smart}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\sigma \sim \text{HalfNormal}(1)$$

$$\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_{\text{smart}} \cdot \text{Smart}_i$$

$$\text{Athletic}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)$$

This model uses the full population. Since `Smart` and `Athletic` were generated independently, the posterior for $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ should be close to zero.

**Model 2 - conditioning on the collider**:

$$\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{smart}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{admitted}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\sigma \sim \text{HalfNormal}(1)$$

$$\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_{\text{smart}} \cdot \text{Smart}_i + \beta_{\text{admitted}} \cdot \text{Admitted}_i$$

$$\text{Athletic}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)$$

This model uses the full population but includes the collider as a covariate. The coefficient $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ is now a conditional association: how `Athletic` changes with `Smart` among observations with the same value of `Admitted`, averaged across the admitted and not-admitted strata by the model.

If the collider logic is correct, the posterior for $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ should move away from zero in the conditioned model, even though `Smart` has no causal effect on `Athletic`.

```{python}
smart_full_t = torch.tensor(population_df["Smart"].values, dtype=torch.float32)
athletic_full_t = torch.tensor(population_df["Athletic"].values, dtype=torch.float32)
admitted_full_t = torch.tensor(population_df["Admitted"].values, dtype=torch.float32)


def smart_athletic_model(smart, athletic, admitted=None):
    alpha = pyro.sample("alpha", dist.Normal(0.0, 1.0))
    beta_smart = pyro.sample("beta_smart", dist.Normal(0.0, 1.0))
    sigma = pyro.sample("sigma", dist.HalfNormal(1.0))

    mu = alpha + beta_smart * smart
    if admitted is not None:
        beta_admitted = pyro.sample("beta_admitted", dist.Normal(0.0, 1.0))
        mu = mu + beta_admitted * admitted

    with pyro.plate("obs", len(athletic)):
        pyro.sample("athletic", dist.Normal(mu, sigma), obs=athletic)


def run_collider_mcmc(smart, athletic, admitted=None, num_samples=1000, warmup_steps=500):
    pyro.clear_param_store()
    mcmc = MCMC(
        NUTS(smart_athletic_model),
        num_samples=num_samples,
        warmup_steps=warmup_steps,
        disable_progbar=True
    )
    mcmc.run(smart, athletic, admitted)
    return mcmc.get_samples()


post_full_collider = run_collider_mcmc(smart_full_t, athletic_full_t)
post_adjusted_collider = run_collider_mcmc(smart_full_t, athletic_full_t, admitted_full_t)
```

```{python}
draws_collider = {
    "Full population\n(no conditioning)": post_full_collider["beta_smart"].detach().numpy(),
    "Full population\n(+ Admitted covariate)": post_adjusted_collider["beta_smart"].detach().numpy(),
}

plot_order_collider = list(draws_collider.keys())
all_collider_vals = np.concatenate(list(draws_collider.values()))
x_lo_c, x_hi_c = np.quantile(all_collider_vals, [0.005, 0.995])
x_pad_c = 0.20 * (x_hi_c - x_lo_c)
x_grid_c = np.linspace(x_lo_c - x_pad_c, x_hi_c + x_pad_c, 400)
ridge_h_c = 0.72

collider_rows, collider_int_rows, collider_summary_rows = [], [], []

for i, name in enumerate(plot_order_collider):
    y_base = len(plot_order_collider) - 1 - i
    draws = draws_collider[name]
    kde = gaussian_kde(draws)
    dens = kde(x_grid_c)
    dens_s = dens / dens.max() * ridge_h_c
    lo, hi = np.quantile(draws, [0.025, 0.975])
    median = np.median(draws)

    for x, d in zip(x_grid_c, dens_s):
        collider_rows.append({"label": name, "x": x, "y_base": y_base, "y": y_base + d})
        if lo <= x <= hi:
            collider_int_rows.append({"label": name, "x": x, "y_base": y_base, "y": y_base + d})

    collider_summary_rows.append({
        "label": name,
        "y_base": y_base,
        "median": median,
        "y_top": y_base + np.interp(median, x_grid_c, dens_s),
    })


def build_polygon_collider(df, order, col="label"):
    rows = []
    for name in order:
        sub = df[df[col] == name].sort_values("x")
        if len(sub) == 0:
            continue
        yb = sub["y_base"].iloc[0]
        rows.extend(sub.to_dict("records"))
        rows += [
            {col: name, "x": sub["x"].iloc[-1], "y_base": yb, "y": yb},
            {col: name, "x": sub["x"].iloc[0], "y_base": yb, "y": yb},
        ]
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


collider_df = pd.DataFrame(collider_rows)
collider_int_df = pd.DataFrame(collider_int_rows)
collider_poly = build_polygon_collider(collider_df, plot_order_collider)
collider_int_poly = build_polygon_collider(collider_int_df, plot_order_collider)
collider_sum_df = pd.DataFrame(collider_summary_rows)

p_collider = (
    ggplot()
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="#d9d9d9", size=1.2)
    + geom_polygon(
        collider_poly,
        aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
        fill="#c8dce8",
        alpha=0.75,
        color=None
    )
    + geom_polygon(
        collider_int_poly,
        aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
        fill="#8fb7cb",
        alpha=0.70,
        color=None
    )
    + geom_line(
        collider_df,
        aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
        color="#002b63",
        size=1.2
    )
    + geom_segment(
        collider_sum_df.assign(x_start=x_grid_c.min(), x_end=x_grid_c.max()),
        aes(x="x_start", xend="x_end", y="y_base", yend="y_base"),
        color="#002b63",
        size=1.0
    )
    + geom_segment(
        collider_sum_df,
        aes(x="median", xend="median", y="y_base", yend="y_top"),
        color="#4b8aa6",
        size=2.3
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        breaks=collider_sum_df["y_base"].tolist(),
        labels=collider_sum_df["label"].tolist(),
        expand=(0.06, 0.25),
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(expand=(0.02, 0.02))
    + coord_cartesian(ylim=(-0.55, len(plot_order_collider) - 0.15))
    + labs(
        title="Conditioning on a collider opens the path",
        subtitle="blue tick = posterior median  ·  shaded = 95% interval",
        x="Coefficient for Smart predicting Athletic",
        y="",
    )
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10.5, 6.8),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill="white", color="white"),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="white", color="white"),
        panel_grid_major=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_line_x=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_line_y=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_ticks_major=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_ticks_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_text_x=element_text(size=12, family="serif", color="#333333"),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=11, family="serif", color="#333333", weight="bold"),
        axis_title_x=element_text(size=12, family="serif", color="#333333", margin={"t": 10}),
        plot_title=element_text(size=24, family="serif", color="black"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=17, family="serif", color="black"),
    )
)

p_collider
```

The two posteriors answer different questions.

In the **full population**, the posterior for $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ is close to zero. This is what we expect, because `Smart` and `Athletic` were generated independently. The path

$$\text{Smart} \rightarrow \text{Admitted} \leftarrow \text{Athletic}$$

is closed until we condition on `Admitted`.

When we **include `Admitted` as a covariate**, the posterior for $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ moves away from zero. This is regression conditioning: the model asks whether `Smart` and `Athletic` are associated after holding admission status fixed. Since admission is a common effect of both traits, holding it fixed opens the collider path. The resulting association is not a causal effect of `Smart` on `Athletic`, or of `Athletic` on `Smart`. It is created by conditioning on their shared outcome.

So the practical answer is:

- If the estimand is the relationship between `Smart` and `Athletic` in the full population, **do not condition on `Admitted`**.
- If the estimand is a descriptive association at fixed admission status, then including `Admitted` as a covariate is conditioning on the collider.

[For a collider, conditioning is not a harmless adjustment. It changes a closed path into an open path and can manufacture an association between two variables that do not cause each other.]{.highlight-key-point}


:::
::::

The fourth and final structure is the **descendant** structure:

![Descendant structure.](img/descendant_structure.png){#fig-descendant-structure width="50%"}

A descendant is any variable that lies downstream of another variable. In the structure here, $D$ is a descendant of the collider $Z$:

$$X \rightarrow Z \leftarrow Y$$

$$Z \rightarrow D$$

Because $D$ is caused by $Z$, learning about $D$ gives us information about $Z$. So even if we do not condition directly on the collider $Z$, conditioning on its descendant $D$ can still open the otherwise closed path between $X$ and $Y$:

$$X \not\!\perp\!\!\!\perp Y \mid D$$


[Conditioning on a descendant of a collider can open the collider path indirectly. The problem is not only conditioning on the shared outcome itself, but also conditioning on variables that reveal information about that shared outcome.]{.highlight-key-point}

::: {.callout-tip icon="false" collapse="true"}
## Intuitive example of a descendant structure

Return to the university admissions example. Suppose `Smart` and `Athletic` are independent traits in the applicant population, but both can help a student get admitted:

$$\text{Smart} \rightarrow \text{Admitted} \leftarrow \text{Athletic}$$

Now add one more variable: whether the student appears in the university's student database. A student appears in that database only after being admitted and enrolling. So database membership is downstream of admission:

$$\text{Admitted} \rightarrow \text{In university database}$$

`Admitted` is still the collider. `In university database` is not itself the collider; it is a downstream consequence of it. If we analyze only people in the database, we are using a variable that carries information about admission, so `Smart` and `Athletic` can still begin to explain away each other:


$$\text{Smart} \not\!\perp\!\!\!\perp \text{Athletic} \mid \text{In university database}$$

[Conditioning on a downstream variable can act like a noisy version of conditioning on the collider itself. The more informative the descendant is about the collider, the more it can induce the same selection-driven association.]{.highlight-key-point}

::: {.callout-note icon="false" collapse="true"}
## Code for the descendant example

Let's simulate some data to illustrate the descendant example. We will create a simple dataset where `Smart` and `Athletic` are independent traits in the applicant population, but both can help a student get admitted. We will then add one more variable: whether the student appears in the university's student database. Database membership is downstream of admission, but it is noisy: some admitted students are missing because it takes time to add them to the system, and a small number of non-admitted applicants can appear because of administrative errors.

![Descendant example DAG.](img/descendant_example.png){#fig-descendant-example width="75%"}

```{python}
# Descendant example: database membership as a noisy proxy for admission
rng_descendant = np.random.default_rng(123)

n_applicants = 5000

# Controls how noisy the descendant is.
# Higher values mean more admitted students are still missing from the database.
database_delay_noise = 0.30

# Optional administrative/data-entry noise.
database_false_positive_rate = 0.01

smart = rng_descendant.normal(0, 1, n_applicants)
athletic = rng_descendant.normal(0, 1, n_applicants)

# Admission is the collider: both traits can help a student clear the threshold.
admission_score = smart + athletic
admission_threshold = np.quantile(admission_score, 0.80)
admitted = (admission_score >= admission_threshold).astype(int)

# Database membership is a descendant of admission.
# Because adding admitted students takes time, some admitted students are not
# yet visible in the database. A small false-positive rate represents occasional
# administrative errors.
p_in_database = np.where(
    admitted == 1,
    1.0 - database_delay_noise,
    database_false_positive_rate,
)
in_university_database = rng_descendant.binomial(1, p_in_database)

descendant_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Smart": smart,
    "Athletic": athletic,
    "Admitted": admitted,
    "InUniversityDatabase": in_university_database,
})

descendant_df.head()

```

#### Summary of the simulated data

The `InUniversityDatabase` variable is a noisy proxy for the `Admitted` variable:

```{python}
descendant_df[["Admitted", "InUniversityDatabase"]].corr().iloc[0, 1]
```

It is not itself the collider, but it is a downstream consequence of it. Let's plot the simulated data to see the associations between `Smart` and `Athletic` in the full population, and when we condition on the `Admitted` variable, and when we condition on the `InUniversityDatabase` variable:


```{python}
from plotnine import (
    ggplot,
    aes,
    geom_point,
    geom_smooth,
    geom_text,
    facet_wrap,
    labs,
    theme_minimal,
    theme,
    element_text,
)

plot_full_df = descendant_df.assign(
    Sample="Full applicant population"
)

plot_admitted_df = descendant_df.query("Admitted == 1").assign(
    Sample="Condition on Admitted"
)

plot_database_df = descendant_df.query("InUniversityDatabase == 1").assign(
    Sample="Condition on Student Database"
)

descendant_plot_df = pd.concat(
    [plot_full_df, plot_admitted_df, plot_database_df],
    ignore_index=True,
)

sample_order = [
    "Full applicant population",
    "Condition on Admitted",
    "Condition on Student Database",
]

descendant_plot_df["Sample"] = pd.Categorical(
    descendant_plot_df["Sample"],
    categories=sample_order,
    ordered=True,
)

descendant_corr_df = (
    descendant_plot_df
    .groupby("Sample", observed=True)
    .apply(
        lambda df: pd.Series({
            "corr": df[["Smart", "Athletic"]].corr().iloc[0, 1],
            "x": df["Smart"].min() + 0.25,
            "y": df["Athletic"].max() - 0.25,
        })
    )
    .reset_index()
)

descendant_corr_df["label"] = descendant_corr_df["corr"].map(
    lambda corr: f"corr = {corr:.2f}"
)

(
    ggplot(descendant_plot_df, aes(x="Smart", y="Athletic"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.25, size=0.8)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", se=False, color="#bf616a")
    + geom_text(
        descendant_corr_df,
        aes(x="x", y="y", label="label"),
        inherit_aes=False,
        ha="left",
        va="top",
        size=9,
    )
    + facet_wrap("~Sample", nrow=1)
    + labs(
        title="Conditioning on a descendant partially opens the collider path",
        subtitle=(
            "Smart and Athletic are independent in the full population, "
            "but become associated after conditioning on Admitted or its noisy descendant."
        ),
        x="Smart",
        y="Athletic",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(12, 4.2),
        plot_title=element_text(weight="bold"),
    )
)
```


#### Analyze the data to see the associations

The descendant example asks a slightly different question from the collider example. We already know that conditioning directly on `Admitted` opens the collider path:

$$\text{Smart} \rightarrow \text{Admitted} \leftarrow \text{Athletic}$$

Now we want to know what happens if we do not condition on `Admitted` itself, but instead condition on a variable downstream of it:

$$\text{Admitted} \rightarrow \text{InUniversityDatabase}$$

Because `InUniversityDatabase` carries information about `Admitted`, conditioning on it can also create an association between `Smart` and `Athletic`.

To illustrate this, we fit three Bayesian linear regressions:

**Model 1 - no conditioning**:

$$\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{smart}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\sigma \sim \text{HalfNormal}(1)$$

$$\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_{\text{smart}} \cdot \text{Smart}_i$$

$$\text{Athletic}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)$$

This model estimates the raw association between `Smart` and `Athletic` in the full applicant population. Since the two traits were generated independently, $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ should be close to zero.

**Model 2 - conditioning on the collider**:

$$\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{smart}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{admitted}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\sigma \sim \text{HalfNormal}(1)$$

$$\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_{\text{smart}} \cdot \text{Smart}_i + \beta_{\text{admitted}} \cdot \text{Admitted}_i$$

$$\text{Athletic}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)$$

This model conditions directly on `Admitted`, the collider.

**Model 3 - conditioning on the descendant**:

$$\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{smart}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\beta_{\text{database}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1)$$

$$\sigma \sim \text{HalfNormal}(1)$$

$$\mu_i = \alpha + \beta_{\text{smart}} \cdot \text{Smart}_i + \beta_{\text{database}} \cdot \text{InUniversityDatabase}_i$$

$$\text{Athletic}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_i,\, \sigma)$$

This model does not condition on the collider directly. Instead, it conditions on `InUniversityDatabase`, a noisy descendant of the collider. If the descendant is informative about admission, the posterior for $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ should move away from zero, but usually less strongly than when conditioning directly on `Admitted`.

```{python}
smart_desc_t = torch.tensor(descendant_df["Smart"].values, dtype=torch.float32)
athletic_desc_t = torch.tensor(descendant_df["Athletic"].values, dtype=torch.float32)
admitted_desc_t = torch.tensor(descendant_df["Admitted"].values, dtype=torch.float32)
database_desc_t = torch.tensor(descendant_df["InUniversityDatabase"].values, dtype=torch.float32)


def descendant_association_model(smart, athletic, condition_var=None):
    alpha = pyro.sample("alpha", dist.Normal(0.0, 1.0))
    beta_smart = pyro.sample("beta_smart", dist.Normal(0.0, 1.0))
    sigma = pyro.sample("sigma", dist.HalfNormal(1.0))

    mu = alpha + beta_smart * smart
    if condition_var is not None:
        beta_condition = pyro.sample("beta_condition", dist.Normal(0.0, 1.0))
        mu = mu + beta_condition * condition_var

    with pyro.plate("obs", len(athletic)):
        pyro.sample("athletic", dist.Normal(mu, sigma), obs=athletic)


def run_descendant_mcmc(smart, athletic, condition_var=None, num_samples=1000, warmup_steps=500):
    pyro.clear_param_store()
    mcmc = MCMC(
        NUTS(descendant_association_model),
        num_samples=num_samples,
        warmup_steps=warmup_steps,
        disable_progbar=True
    )
    mcmc.run(smart, athletic, condition_var)
    return mcmc.get_samples()


post_desc_unconditioned = run_descendant_mcmc(smart_desc_t, athletic_desc_t)
post_desc_admitted = run_descendant_mcmc(smart_desc_t, athletic_desc_t, admitted_desc_t)
post_desc_database = run_descendant_mcmc(smart_desc_t, athletic_desc_t, database_desc_t)
```

```{python}
draws_descendant = {
    "Full population\n(no conditioning)": post_desc_unconditioned["beta_smart"].detach().numpy(),
    "Full population\n(+ Admitted covariate)": post_desc_admitted["beta_smart"].detach().numpy(),
    "Full population\n(+ Database covariate)": post_desc_database["beta_smart"].detach().numpy(),
}

plot_order_descendant = list(draws_descendant.keys())
all_descendant_vals = np.concatenate(list(draws_descendant.values()))
x_lo_d, x_hi_d = np.quantile(all_descendant_vals, [0.005, 0.995])
x_pad_d = 0.20 * (x_hi_d - x_lo_d)
x_grid_d = np.linspace(x_lo_d - x_pad_d, x_hi_d + x_pad_d, 400)
ridge_h_d = 0.72

descendant_rows, descendant_int_rows, descendant_summary_rows = [], [], []

for i, name in enumerate(plot_order_descendant):
    y_base = len(plot_order_descendant) - 1 - i
    draws = draws_descendant[name]
    kde = gaussian_kde(draws)
    dens = kde(x_grid_d)
    dens_s = dens / dens.max() * ridge_h_d
    lo, hi = np.quantile(draws, [0.025, 0.975])
    median = np.median(draws)

    for x, d in zip(x_grid_d, dens_s):
        descendant_rows.append({"label": name, "x": x, "y_base": y_base, "y": y_base + d})
        if lo <= x <= hi:
            descendant_int_rows.append({"label": name, "x": x, "y_base": y_base, "y": y_base + d})

    descendant_summary_rows.append({
        "label": name,
        "y_base": y_base,
        "median": median,
        "y_top": y_base + np.interp(median, x_grid_d, dens_s),
    })


def build_polygon_descendant(df, order, col="label"):
    rows = []
    for name in order:
        sub = df[df[col] == name].sort_values("x")
        if len(sub) == 0:
            continue
        yb = sub["y_base"].iloc[0]
        rows.extend(sub.to_dict("records"))
        rows += [
            {col: name, "x": sub["x"].iloc[-1], "y_base": yb, "y": yb},
            {col: name, "x": sub["x"].iloc[0], "y_base": yb, "y": yb},
        ]
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


descendant_df_ridge = pd.DataFrame(descendant_rows)
descendant_int_df_ridge = pd.DataFrame(descendant_int_rows)
descendant_poly = build_polygon_descendant(descendant_df_ridge, plot_order_descendant)
descendant_int_poly = build_polygon_descendant(descendant_int_df_ridge, plot_order_descendant)
descendant_sum_df = pd.DataFrame(descendant_summary_rows)

p_descendant = (
    ggplot()
    + geom_vline(xintercept=0, color="#d9d9d9", size=1.2)
    + geom_polygon(
        descendant_poly,
        aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
        fill="#c8dce8",
        alpha=0.75,
        color=None
    )
    + geom_polygon(
        descendant_int_poly,
        aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
        fill="#8fb7cb",
        alpha=0.70,
        color=None
    )
    + geom_line(
        descendant_df_ridge,
        aes(x="x", y="y", group="label"),
        color="#002b63",
        size=1.2
    )
    + geom_segment(
        descendant_sum_df.assign(x_start=x_grid_d.min(), x_end=x_grid_d.max()),
        aes(x="x_start", xend="x_end", y="y_base", yend="y_base"),
        color="#002b63",
        size=1.0
    )
    + geom_segment(
        descendant_sum_df,
        aes(x="median", xend="median", y="y_base", yend="y_top"),
        color="#4b8aa6",
        size=2.3
    )
    + scale_y_continuous(
        breaks=descendant_sum_df["y_base"].tolist(),
        labels=descendant_sum_df["label"].tolist(),
        expand=(0.06, 0.25),
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(expand=(0.02, 0.02))
    + coord_cartesian(ylim=(-0.55, len(plot_order_descendant) - 0.15))
    + labs(
        title="Conditioning on a descendant can open the collider path",
        subtitle="blue tick = posterior median  ·  shaded = 95% interval",
        x="Coefficient for Smart predicting Athletic",
        y="",
    )
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10.5, 6.8),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill="white", color="white"),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="white", color="white"),
        panel_grid_major=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_line_x=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_line_y=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_ticks_major=element_line(color="black", size=0.8),
        axis_ticks_minor=element_blank(),
        axis_text_x=element_text(size=12, family="serif", color="#333333"),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=11, family="serif", color="#333333", weight="bold"),
        axis_title_x=element_text(size=12, family="serif", color="#333333", margin={"t": 10}),
        plot_title=element_text(size=24, family="serif", color="black"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=17, family="serif", color="black"),
    )
)

p_descendant
```

The three posteriors show how the association changes as we condition on different variables.

In the **full population**, the posterior for $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ is close to zero. This is the baseline result: `Smart` and `Athletic` were generated independently, so knowing a student's value of `Smart` tells us essentially nothing about their value of `Athletic`.

When we **include `Admitted` as a covariate**, the posterior for $\beta_{\text{smart}}$ moves away from zero. This is the same collider effect as before. The model is now estimating the association between `Smart` and `Athletic` after holding fixed a shared outcome of both traits.

When we **include `InUniversityDatabase` as a covariate**, the posterior also moves away from zero. This is the descendant effect. The model has not conditioned on the collider itself, but it has conditioned on a variable that contains information about the collider. Because database membership is downstream of admission, it partially reveals admission status, and that is enough to partially open the path between `Smart` and `Athletic`.

The strength of this induced association depends on how informative the descendant is. If `InUniversityDatabase` were a perfect copy of `Admitted`, conditioning on it would behave almost exactly like conditioning on `Admitted`. If it were pure noise and carried no information about admission, conditioning on it would not open the path.

So the practical answer to the question of on which variables to condition is:

- If the estimand is the relationship between `Smart` and `Athletic` in the full applicant population, **do not condition on `Admitted`**.
- Also avoid conditioning on variables downstream of `Admitted`, such as `InUniversityDatabase`, unless the estimand specifically requires it.
- A descendant of a collider can be dangerous because it can look like an ordinary observed covariate while still carrying information about the collider.

[Conditioning on a descendant of a collider is a weaker, noisier version of conditioning on the collider itself. It can still manufacture an association between variables that were independent in the population.]{.highlight-key-point}

:::

:::

##### Identifying the four path structures in the DEL DAG

Before writing the statistical model, we can use the four elemental path structures to describe the causal assumptions in @fig-dag-with-latent-factors-and-replicates.

1.  **Forks (common causes).** Input abundance [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"} is a common cause of the library, matrix, and target counts, producing the forks

    -   [$C_{l,i}$]{style="color:#b48ead"} ← [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"} → [$C_{m,i,r}$]{style="color:#5e81ac"},
    -   [$C_{l,i}$]{style="color:#b48ead"} ← [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"} → [$C_{t,i,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"}, and
    -   [$C_{m,i,r}$]{style="color:#5e81ac"} ← [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"} → [$C_{t,i,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"}.

    Matrix background [$b_i$]{style="color:#88c0d0"} creates another fork:

    -   [$C_{m,i,r}$]{style="color:#5e81ac"} ← [$b_i$]{style="color:#88c0d0"} → [$C_{t,i,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"}.

    These shared causes can induce associations among the observed counts. They are not, however, confounders of the path [$s_i$]{style="color:#bf616a"} → [$C_{t,i,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"}, because neither [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"} nor [$b_i$]{style="color:#88c0d0"} causes [$s_i$]{style="color:#bf616a"}.

2.  **Pipes (mediation).** No mediation structure appears in this DAG. Every latent process acts directly on an observed count, and no node lies on a directed causal path between another cause and its count. The DAG therefore contains no mediator to condition on.

3.  **Colliders (common effects).** The target count [$C_{t,i,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"} is a shared effect of [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"}, [$b_i$]{style="color:#88c0d0"}, [$s_i$]{style="color:#bf616a"}, and [$\gamma_{t,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"}. It is therefore a collider for every pair of these causes. For example,

    -   [$s_i$]{style="color:#bf616a"} → [$C_{t,i,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"} ← [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"}.

    Similarly, the matrix count [$C_{m,i,r}$]{style="color:#5e81ac"} is a collider for [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"}, [$b_i$]{style="color:#88c0d0"}, and [$\gamma_{m,r}$]{style="color:#5e81ac"}. Conditioning on, or selecting observations by, either count can induce associations among its otherwise independent causes.

4.  **Descendants of colliders.** The observed counts are descendants of their latent causes, but neither collider, [$C_{m,i,r}$]{style="color:#5e81ac"} nor [$C_{t,i,r}$]{style="color:#d08770"}, has a downstream node in this DAG. Consequently, the specific descendant-of-a-collider structure described earlier is absent.

[The DAG contains common causes and colliders, but no mediators or descendants of colliders. This supports a generative model in which input abundance, matrix background, target-specific enrichment, and replicate effects enter as distinct causes of the observed counts, rather than treating the counts as interchangeable adjustment variables.]{.highlight-key-point}

### Write the generative model and priors

The DAG tells us which quantities should contribute to each count, but we still need to choose how those contributions combine. We begin with a hierarchical Negative Binomial model. This is a useful first model because DEL counts are non-negative, sparse, and overdispersed, while the hierarchy allows information to be shared across compounds without forcing them to have the same abundance, background retention, or target enrichment.

Let $K_l$, $K_{m,r}$, and $K_{t,r}$ denote the total usable reads in the library, matrix ($r$th replicate), and target ($r$th replicate), and let $N$ be the number of compounds. We use

$$
E_l = \frac{K_l}{N}, \qquad
E_{m,r} = \frac{K_{m,r}}{N}, \qquad
E_{t,r} = \frac{K_{t,r}}{N}
$$

as known sequencing-exposure offsets. **Each is the count expected for one compound if reads were distributed uniformly in that sample**. Using these offsets removes differences in sequencing depth from the latent compound effects; it does not assume that the compounds are actually equally abundant.

#### Likelihood and link function

The likelihood describes how the latent biological and technical processes become noisy observed counts. For compound $i$ and replicate $r$, we specify

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{l,i} &\sim \operatorname{NegBinomial}_2(\mu_{l,i}, \phi_l), \\
C_{m,i,r} &\sim \operatorname{NegBinomial}_2(\mu_{m,i,r}, \phi_m), \\
C_{t,i,r} &\sim \operatorname{NegBinomial}_2(\mu_{t,i,r}, \phi_t),
\end{aligned}
$$

Each line says that the observed count is a noisy realisation around an expected count $\mu$. One way to think about this is that $\mu$ is the model's recipe for repeated versions of the experiment, whereas $C$ is the particular result obtained on one occasion. Repeating the same experiment does not have to produce exactly $\mu$ reads, but the results should be distributed around it.

The subscript in $\operatorname{NegBinomial}_2$ identifies the **NB2 parameterisation** of the Negative Binomial family; it does not denote a different distribution. The notation follows [Cameron and Trivedi (1986)](https://doi.org/10.1002/jae.3950010104). In NB2, the extra-Poisson variance grows quadratically with the mean:

$$
\mathbb{E}[C \mid \mu,\phi] = \mu,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(C \mid \mu,\phi) = \mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\phi}.
$$

This differs from NB1, where the additional variance grows linearly with the mean. The distinction matters for sequencing data because high-abundance compounds can be much more variable in absolute terms than low-abundance compounds. For example, if $\mu=10$ and $\phi=5$, NB2 gives

$$
\operatorname{Var}(C)=10+\frac{10^2}{5}=30,
$$

rather than the variance of $10$ implied by a Poisson model. Smaller values of $\phi$ correspond to greater overdispersion, while $\phi \rightarrow \infty$ approaches the Poisson variance. We use separate parameters $\phi_l$, $\phi_m$, and $\phi_t$ because the library, matrix, and target experiments may have different amounts of residual variation.

The next step is to specify the expected counts. Since an expected count must be positive, it would be awkward to model $\mu$ directly with an ordinary additive equation: some combinations of effects could produce a negative value. A **log link** solves this problem by modelling $\log\mu$, which can take any real value, while guaranteeing that $\mu=\exp(\log\mu)$ remains positive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \mu_{l,i}
    &= \log E_l + \alpha_l + \textcolor{#a3be8c}{a_i}, \\
\log \mu_{m,i,r}
    &= \log E_{m,r} + \alpha_m + \textcolor{#a3be8c}{a_i} + \textcolor{#88c0d0}{b_i} + \gamma_{m,r}, \\
\log \mu_{t,i,r}
    &= \log E_{t,r} + \alpha_t + \textcolor{#a3be8c}{a_i} + \textcolor{#88c0d0}{b_i} + \textcolor{#bf616a}{s_i} + \gamma_{t,r}.
\end{aligned}
$$

On the log scale, the terms are added. Exponentiating the equations reveals their more intuitive count-scale interpretation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{l,i}
    &= E_l \times e^{\alpha_l} \times e^{a_i}, \\
\mu_{m,i,r}
    &= E_{m,r} \times e^{\alpha_m} \times e^{a_i} \times e^{b_i} \times e^{\gamma_{m,r}}, \\
\mu_{t,i,r}
    &= E_{t,r} \times e^{\alpha_t} \times e^{a_i} \times e^{b_i} \times e^{s_i} \times e^{\gamma_{t,r}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The model can therefore be imagined as a sequence of multiplicative adjustment knobs. We start with the sequencing exposure $E$ and then turn each relevant knob up or down. An effect of zero on the log scale is a multiplier of $e^0=1$, so it leaves the expected count unchanged. A positive effect increases the count and a negative effect decreases it. For example, an effect of $\log 2$ doubles the expected count, while an effect of $\log(1/2)$ halves it.

This factorisation is the statistical translation of the DAG:

- The input-abundance multiplier $e^{a_i}$ appears in all three equations because [$a_i$]{style="color:#a3be8c"} affects every condition.
- The matrix-background multiplier $e^{b_i}$ appears in the matrix and target equations because [$b_i$]{style="color:#88c0d0"} affects both post-selection conditions.
- The target-specific multiplier $e^{s_i}$ appears only in the target equation because [$s_i$]{style="color:#bf616a"} represents enrichment specific to the target.
- The replicate multipliers $e^{\gamma_{m,r}}$ and $e^{\gamma_{t,r}}$ allow otherwise comparable experiments to have different recovery efficiencies.

The terms $\log E_l$, $\log E_{m,r}$, and $\log E_{t,r}$ are sequencing-depth offsets. They account for the fact that a sample with more total reads should produce larger counts for every compound; for example, doubling $E$ doubles the expected count when all other terms are fixed. After this depth effect has been included, the condition intercepts $\alpha_l$, $\alpha_m$, and $\alpha_t$ capture remaining condition-wide differences in average recovery between the library, matrix, and target experiments. The replicate effects $\gamma_{m,r}$ and $\gamma_{t,r}$ then capture sample-specific departures from the matrix and target baselines.

As a worked example, suppose a target replicate has $E_{t,r}=20$ reads per compound and the relevant multipliers are

$$
e^{\alpha_t}=1.1, \qquad
e^{a_i}=1.5, \qquad
e^{b_i}=2, \qquad
e^{s_i}=3, \qquad
e^{\gamma_{t,r}}=0.9.
$$

The expected target count is then

$$
\mu_{t,i,r}
=20\times1.1\times1.5\times2\times3\times0.9
=178.2.
$$

Without target-specific enrichment, $s_i=0$ and therefore $e^{s_i}=1$, giving an expected count of $59.4$. In this example, the target-specific term triples the expected target count after the model has already accounted for sequencing depth, input abundance, matrix background, condition-wide recovery, and replicate variation. The observed count need not equal either value exactly: the Negative Binomial likelihood represents the remaining sampling and experimental noise around the expectation.

More generally, $e^{s_i}$ is the compound's target-specific fold change relative to the typical compound, conditional on the other terms in the model. Equivalently, increasing $s_i$ by one multiplies the expected target count by $e \approx 2.72$.

#### Identifiable parameterisation

The intercepts and latent effects would not be separately identified if an arbitrary constant could be moved from one to the other. We therefore define the compound effects and replicate effects as deviations around zero:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i =
\sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i =
\sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i = 0,
\qquad
\sum_{r=1}^{R_m} \gamma_{m,r} =
\sum_{r=1}^{R_t} \gamma_{t,r} = 0.
$$

In computation, these constraints can be imposed with centred raw parameters. For example,

$$
a_i = \sigma_a(z_{a,i}-\bar z_a),
\qquad z_{a,i} \sim \operatorname{Normal}(0,1),
$$

with analogous definitions for $b_i$, $s_i$, $\gamma_{m,r}$, and $\gamma_{t,r}$. This non-centred hierarchical parameterisation also tends to be more stable when some compounds have little information.

The zero-sum definition makes an important point about the estimand explicit: $s_i=0$ means **typical target-specific enrichment within this experiment**, not a physical guarantee of no binding. Positive values indicate more target-specific enrichment than the library average, and negative values indicate less. Identifying an absolute no-binding state would require additional information, such as known non-binders, a calibrated negative-control distribution, or a mixture model with an explicitly defined null component.

#### Priors

We use weakly informative priors on the log scale:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_l,\alpha_m,\alpha_t
    &\sim \operatorname{Normal}(0,1), \\
\sigma_a
    &\sim \operatorname{HalfNormal}(0,0.75), \\
\sigma_b
    &\sim \operatorname{HalfNormal}(0,0.75), \\
\sigma_s
    &\sim \operatorname{HalfNormal}(0,0.50), \\
\sigma_{\gamma_m},\sigma_{\gamma_t}
    &\sim \operatorname{HalfNormal}(0,0.25).
\end{aligned}
$$

The latent effects are then generated by

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{a,i},z_{b,i},z_{s,i} &\sim \operatorname{Normal}(0,1), \\
z_{\gamma_m,r},z_{\gamma_t,r} &\sim \operatorname{Normal}(0,1),
\end{aligned}
$$

followed by the centred transformations described above. These priors regularise noisy compound-level estimates towards the population average while still allowing the data to identify large effects. The somewhat tighter prior for $\sigma_s$ reflects the expectation that most compounds do not have extreme target-specific enrichment. It is not intended to rule out strong binders: a log effect of $1$ already corresponds to a $2.7$-fold change, and the hierarchical scale can increase when the full collection of compounds supports greater heterogeneity.

For dispersion, it is easier to place priors on the asymptotic extra-Poisson coefficient of variation,

$$
\kappa_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\phi_c}},
\qquad c \in \{l,m,t\},
$$

because $\kappa_c$ is directly interpretable: for large $\mu$, the Negative Binomial coefficient of variation approaches $\kappa_c$. We use

$$
\kappa_l,\kappa_m,\kappa_t
\sim \operatorname{HalfNormal}(0,0.50),
\qquad
\phi_c = \kappa_c^{-2}.
$$

This prior includes values close to the Poisson limit while allowing substantial overdispersion. It is preferable to an arbitrary diffuse prior directly on $\phi$, whose practical implications are difficult to see.

These numerical scales are starting assumptions, not facts learned from the observed counts. Their consequences must be evaluated by simulating complete library, matrix, and target datasets from the prior predictive distribution. In particular, we should check whether the model produces plausible zero frequencies, upper tails, replicate correlations, and target-to-matrix fold changes. If it does not, the priors should be revised before fitting the observed data.

The model also makes several substantive assumptions that should later be tested by sensitivity analysis: background transfers from matrix to target with the same log-scale contribution $b_i$; counts are conditionally independent once the latent effects are known; and a single Negative Binomial dispersion is shared by all compounds within a condition. More flexible versions could estimate a matrix-to-target loading, use a sparse or heavy-tailed prior for $s_i$, or replace the Negative Binomial likelihood with a Poisson-lognormal model. Those extensions should be motivated by failed predictive checks rather than added automatically.

[The target signal is the posterior distribution of a log fold-change, not a corrected count. The model infers it by jointly explaining all observed counts while propagating uncertainty in input abundance, matrix background, replicate effects, and overdispersion.]{.highlight-key-point}

## References:

- [Rosenbaum & Rubin (1983)](https://academic.oup.com/jrsssb/article/45/2/212/7027731) — classic paper on sensitivity to an unobserved binary covariate with a binary outcome.

- [VanderWeele & Ding (2017), E-value](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28693043/) — a very accessible modern approach: it quantifies how strongly an unmeasured confounder would need to be associated with both treatment and outcome to explain away an association.

- [Cinelli & Hazlett (2020), “Making Sense of Sensitivity”](https://academic.oup.com/jrsssb/article/82/1/39/7056023?guestAccessKey=) — especially useful for regression-style intuition. Their “robustness value” asks how strong omitted confounding would need to be to overturn a conclusion.

- [sensemakr](https://carloscinelli.com/sensemakr/) — practical R/Stata implementation of Cinelli & Hazlett’s approach; useful if you want a computational reference rather than only theory
